name: Float Glass Laser Cleaning Dataset
description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for Float Glass. Includes thermal, optical, mechanical, and laser interaction properties validated against industry standards.

license.name: Creative Commons Attribution 4.0 International
license.description: Free to share and adapt with attribution

measurementTechnique: Laser ablation testing, material characterization, spectroscopy

includedInDataCatalog.name: Z-Beam Material Properties Database
includedInDataCatalog.description: Comprehensive laser cleaning parameters and material properties for industrial applications

powerRange.description: Optimal average power for Float Glass surface cleaning without thermal damage

wavelength.description: Near-IR wavelength optimized for Float Glass absorption and minimal subsurface damage

spotSize.description: Beam spot diameter for precise cleaning with adequate energy density

repetitionRate.description: Optimal repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage and thermal management

energyDensity.description: Fluence threshold for effective contaminant removal below glass damage threshold

pulseWidth.description: Nanosecond pulse duration for effective contaminant removal without glass damage

scanSpeed.description: Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage

passCount.description: Number of cleaning passes for complete contaminant removal

overlapRatio.description: Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without excessive thermal buildup

dwellTime.description: Effective dwell time per spot for contaminant ablation

power.description: Typical range for industrial 1064 nm fiber lasers in glass cleaning to balance efficiency and safety; starting at 200 W for contamination removal without overheating. Max safe below 500 W to avoid thermal stress on float glass (melting point ~500°C). Source: IPG Photonics specs and studies in Optics and Lasers in Engineering (2018).

scan_speed.description: Effective speeds for 1064 nm cleaning; slower (500-1000 mm/s) for heavy contamination, faster (up to 5000 mm/s) for light residues. Recommended 2000 mm/s for optimal contaminant removal on float glass while minimizing heat accumulation. Source: Industry standards from Laser Cleaning Europe guidelines and J. Cleaner Production (2020).

pulse_width.description: Nanosecond regime preferred for float glass to enable selective ablation of contaminants with minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ); picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but costlier. 20 ns recommended to limit thermal diffusion in low-conductivity glass. Source: Laser physics research in Applied Surface Science (2019).

repetition_rate.description: Typical for pulsed cleaning; higher rates (50-100 kHz) speed up process but risk heat buildup on reflective float glass. 50 kHz balances speed and safety for contamination removal. Trade-off: High freq increases overlap heating. Source: Academic literature in Optics Express (2017).

energy_density.description: Fluence range for safe cleaning below float glass damage threshold (~3 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to partial IR absorption). 0.5 J/cm² recommended for contaminant removal without substrate ablation or cracking. Source: Ablation threshold studies in Journal of Applied Physics (2021).

spot_size.description: Beam diameter range for precision (100-300 μm) vs. large-area cleaning (up to 1000 μm); 300 μm recommended for uniform coverage on float glass surfaces without excessive energy concentration. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent Inc. and industry manuals.

pass_count.description: Single pass for light contamination, multi-pass (2-5) for heavy layers; 2 passes recommended for complete removal on float glass to ensure uniformity without cumulative damage. Source: Industry case studies in Surface and Coatings Technology (2022).

overlap_ratio.description: Ensures uniform coverage; 50% recommended for float glass to avoid gaps or overexposure, balancing efficiency and preventing thermal gradients. Typical 30-80% in optimization. Source: Process studies in Lasers in Manufacturing and Materials Processing (2019).
